Please confirm that your Cellular DACT, with a phone line secondary, transmitter is configured as described, per NFPA 72, 2016 edition, section 26.6.4.1.4 (B): The following requirements shall apply to all combinations listed in 26.6.4.1.4(A): (1) The means for supervising each channel shall be in a manner approved for the method of transmission employed. (2) If a signal has not been processed over the subject channel in the previous 6 hours, a test signal shall be processed. (3) The failure of either channel shall send a trouble signal on the other channel within 4 minutes. (4) When one transmission channel has failed, all status change signals shall be sent over the other channel. (5) The primary channel shall be capable of delivering an indication to the DACT that the message has been received by the supervising station. (6)*The first attempt to send a status change signal shall use the primary channel. Exception: When the primary channel is known to have failed. (7) Simultaneous transmission over both channels shall be permitted. (8) Failure of telephone lines (numbers) shall be annunciated locally.

NFPA 72, 2016 Edition, 10.6.5.1.2 The branch circuit supplying the fire alarm equipment or emergency communication system(s) shall supply no other loads. A key requirement is that primary power must be supplied by a dedicated branch circuit. The intent is that no other system or equipment can be powered from the same circuit. So, for example, it would be improper to install an electrical receptacle outlet on the circuit for technicians to use when servicing the system. It is acceptable to remove and/or securely cover the additional outlets in the box near the control so no other device can be plugged into an open outlet.

1 . The issuing company is responsible for all aspects of Central Station service, runner response and periodic inspection and testing and maintenance, as described in NFPA 72, section 26.3.2. AMENDED REPORT ITEMS - A1) In the signal event history provided for this account, on 1/28/23 at 4:05:36pm five Fire alarm signals were simultaneously received. These fire alarm signals were accessed by an operator at 4:07:15pm (99 seconds elapsed time). Per NFPA 72 section 26.2.1.2, all fire alarm signals received at the supervising station shall be immediately retransmitted to the communications center. Per NFPA 72 (2016 ed.) section A.26.2.1.2 The term immediately in this context is intended to mean "without reasonable delay." Routine handling should take a maximum of 90 seconds from receipt of an alarm signal. The recoded time the Fire department was contacted is 4:08:48pm (3 minutes and 12 seconds after alarm receipt). A2) The station's operator first attempt to dispatch to the fire department is recorded at 4:07:32pm. It appears to be an incorrect phone number and a second call recorded at 4:08:48 that indicates Fire dispatch answered the call. Per NFPA 72 (2019 ed.) sections 14.5.5 to 14.5.7, when the retransmission means is the public-switched telephone network, testing shall be at weekly intervals to confirm its operation to each communication center and this testing is to be recorded at the central station. Please provided central station records that indicate a call is made at least a weekly frequency to the communication centers that have alarm retransmission signals sent. A3) Per NFPA 72 (2016 ed.) section 26.3.8.1.1(2), After retransmission of the alarm signal, the station shall dispatch a runner/technician to the premise to provide the necessary resetting of equipment and for placing the system back in operating condition. There is no indication that a service runner was dispatched or arrived at the premise. A4) The signal history indicates that at 5:45:04pm the previous alarm zones had not restored. There is no later indication that these zones were restored. Per NFPA 72 (2016 ed.) section 26.2.4, All supervising station fire alarm systems shall be programmed to report restoral signals and any signal that has not restored to normal within 24 hours shall be redisplayed to an operator as a non-restored signal and shall be reported to the subscriber. Please provide a cause analysis for the above noncompliance items and actions taken to prevent future noncompliance in signal processing and response.

Moving forward with all properties that will require a UL Certificate for central station service will require the following: The issuing company is responsible for all aspects of Central Station service, runner response and periodic inspection and testing and maintenance, as described in NFPA 72, section 26.3.2. This shall be provided for under contract between the certificate issuing company (the prime contractor) and the protected premise owner (or his designated representative) per the 2016 edition of NFPA 72, section 26.3.3. To maintain the issuance of this certificate your Service Center will need a contract with the owner's representative for performing inspections, maintenance, and runner service, including a subcontract agreement with a UL Listed monitoring station. NFPA 72, 2016 Edition, Paragraphs 26.3.2 and 26.3.3 Service Scope. Section 26.3 shall apply to central station service, which consists of the following elements: (1) Installation of alarm transmitters (2) Alarm, guard, supervisory, and trouble signal monitoring (3) Retransmission (4) Associated record keeping and reporting (5) Testing and maintenance (6) Runner service Contract Requirements. The central station service elements shall be provided under contract to a subscriber by a prime contractor that has a listing for central station fire alarm services. **No contracts were available for review at the time of audit. Please provide copies of executed contracts with your clearance letter.

Upon receipt of trouble signals or other signals pertaining solely to matters of equipment maintenance of the alarm systems, the central station shall perform the following actions: (1)*Communicate immediately with persons designated by the subscriber (2) Dispatch personnel to arrive within 4 hours to initiate maintenance, if necessary (3) When the interruption is more than 8 hours, provide notice to the subscriber and the fire department if so required by the Authority Having Jurisdiction as to the nature of the interruption, the time of occurrence, and the restoration of service. **Signal History was reviewed from 12/13/22 to 3/13/23. Throughout that time frame, there were numerous signals indicating loss of communication. The records indicate no runner notification nor arriving within 4 hours. NFPA 72. 2016 Edition, Section 3.3.282.1 Central Station Service. The use of a system or a group of systems including the protected premises fire alarm system(s) in which the operations of circuits and devices are signaled to, recorded in, and supervised from a listed central station that has competent and experienced operators who, upon receipt of a signal, take such action as required by this Code. Related activities at the protected premises, such as equipment installation, inspection, testing, maintenance, and runner service, are the responsibility of the central station or a listed alarm service local company. Central station service is controlled and operated by a person, firm, or corporation whose business is the furnishing of such contracted services or whose properties are the protected premises. Please provide copy for review.

Upon arrival at the premise, the Fire Alarm Control Panel was in alarm condition and the alarm silenced. RTU unit 1 had tripped the FACP. In reviewing the signal history for this system, it is noted that around the end of January there was an alarm signal handled by the monitoring station. However, there we not records to indicate that a runner was ever dispatched. It would be the case that the monitoring station was not informed that this system was to be handled as a central station service account. To resolve these items in your clearance letter, please work with your monitoring station in providing an action plan to address the following: Ensure the station's ability to dispatch runner or service persons to arrive on premise within the response time required, including a monitoring station continued response when it is determined the runner dispatch has failed (Ref NFPA 72 section 26.3.8.1.1.(2), 2016 edition).

Upon audit review, the issued UL Certificate indicates the fire alarm system declaration to be Proprietary Supervising Station Service; however, the installed system service does not meet NFPA 72 confines for Proprietary Supervising Station Service as the owner is neither qualified or UL Listed to provide inspection, testing and maintenance nor is the owner providing Runner Service - The system does however meet the confines of Remote Station Service - Please edit and reissue the certificate to accurately declare the appropriate Supervising Station Alarm Systems Service (NFPA 72-2016: 3.3.282.2 / 26.4 / 3.3.282.3 / 26.5).

We understand several buildings on the Kaiser campus employ different types of fire alarm control manufacture's - Siemens, Notifier and the Honeywell XLS3000 system. The Honeywell system was originally documented as the primary control unit for Kaiser's Proprietary Supervising Station listing. The Siemens and Notifier premise control units were installed at different buildings other than the Kaiser original listing. The documented Kaiser building/area housing the Honeywell XLS3000 was employed for alerting the operators of abnormal conditions and the like. Off premise signals from the above control units were being transmitted to listed monitoring station General Monitoring Services Inc., (GMS) UL file S7894. This listed central station handles retransmission of alarm signals to the communication center (such as 911), and direct responding fire engines to the Kaiser campus. GMS also maintains all signal associated record keeping. Same outputs from the Siemens and Notifier control units that transmit signals to GMS also trigger inputs to the Honeywell XLS3000 that which alerts the Kaiser operators of any fire alarm condition. In addition to GMS handling the fire alarm signal, the Kaiser operators dispatch their own emergency response team to meet responders at the designated Kaiser Emergency Command Center on campus. Unfortunately the above current operational procedure does not meet NFPA 72 2016 Edition, Proprietary Supervising Station Alarm Systems par 26.4.6.6, which specifically states - 26.4.6.6.1 Alarms. Upon receipt of an alarm signal, the proprietary supervising station operator shall initiate action to perform the following: (1) Notify the communications center, the emergency response team, and such other parties as the authority having jurisdiction requires in accordance with 26.2.1 (2) Dispatch a runner or technician to the alarm location to arrive within 2 hours after receipt of a signal (3) Restore the system as soon as possible after disposition of the cause of the alarm signal Par 26.6.1 The intent of a listed proprietary supervising station is to include the responsibility of handling signals from premise protected properties under the control/ownership of Kaiser, and retransmission in accordance with NFPA 72 2016 Edition par 26.2.1. The original listing described the facility hosting the supervising station and equipment Honeywell XLS3000 serving as the life safety system. Since Kaiser now has multiple buildings employing different FACU and utilizing a separate monitoring station for handling off-premise signals, by way of communication center (911) or PSAP, and record keeping is out of the scheme of proprietary supervising station service. Instead, this model may comply with central station fire service. In view of the above, and if acceptable to the AHJ, it may be advisable to the replace your UUKA listing with UUFX (central station fire alarm service) category listing. I believe you have full time employees with the necessary technical competency to achieve NFPA 72 compliance. The other option requires moving all monitoring/dispatching responsibility back to Kaiser and continue with the proprietary supervising station listing. Maintaining your UUKA listing would require verification of equipment supervision/operation compliance (NFPA 72 2016 Edition par 26.5.1) with other control units adjunct with the Honeywell XLS3000.

NFPA 72-2016 Table 14.4.3.2 (9) (5) 3 Yeartest Replace the battery or conduct a load test of the battery capacity. Load test the battery based on the manufacturer’s specifications for a discharge rate of 3 hours or more by applying the current indicated for the selected hourly discharge rate continuously, until the terminal voltage decreases to the end voltage specified by the manufacturer. Record the test duration and calculate the battery capacity including adjustment for ambient temperature. Replace the battery if capacity is less than or equal to 80% or at the next scheduled test interval if battery capacity is less than 85%. *** Batteries dated 2018 and have pasted the manufacture recommended replacement date. Per NFPA 72 section 26.6.4.1.4 (A) (2016 edition ) A system employing a DACT shall employ one telephone line (number). In addition, one of the following transmission means shall be employed: (1) One-way private radio alarm system (2) Two-way RF multiplex system (3) Transmission means complying with 26.6.3 Exception: Where access to two technologies in the preceding list is not available at the protected premises, with the approval of the authority having jurisdiction, a telephone line (number) shall be permitted to be used as the second transmission means. Each DACT shall be programmed to call a second DACR line (number) when the signal transmission sequence to the first called line (number) is unsuccessful. The DACT shall be capable of selecting the operable means of transmission in the event of failure of the other means. Where two telephone lines (numbers) are used, it shall be permitted to test each telephone line (number) at alternating 6-hour intervals. A review of the signal history shows that the two DACT phone lines only test alternately every 24 hours. They need to test every 6 hours, alternately.

For all future installations; please advise the installation and service personnel that electrical equipment panels, power supplies, FACU's and the like, should be installed so the top of the enclosure is no more than 6 1/2 feet above grade. Ref. NFPA 70, NEC 2017 Ed. Article 110.26 (A) Working Space. Working space for equipment operating at 1000 volts, nominal, or less to ground and likely to require examination, adjustment, servicing, or maintenance while energized shall comply with the dimensions of 110.26(A) 1), (A)(2), (A)(3), and (A)(4) or as required or permitted elsewhere in this Code. Ref. Article 110.26 (A)(3) Height of Working Space. The work space shall be clear and extend from the grade, floor, or platform to a height of 2.0 m (61∕2 ft.) or the height of the equipment, whichever is greater. Within the height requirements of this section, other equipment that is associated with the electrical installation and is located above or below the electrical equipment shall be permitted to extend not more than 150 mm (6 in.) beyond the front of the electrical equipment.

NFPA 70, 2017 Edition, Article 110.12 (A) Unused Openings. It is understood that the water mist fire suppression systems are expected to engage the releasing solenoid after two smoke detectors in the area covered are in an alarm condition. However, there are systems with multiple small enclosed rooms which only have one smoke detector installed. For the system to properly function, either a second smoke detector needs to be added or the system be permitted to be energized on just the one smoke detector NFPA 72, 2019 edition, table 14.4.3.2 (18)(8) and section 23.11.7.

Per UL 827, 8th Edition, Par. 8.1.2: Where an automation system or receivers are located in a separate room within or outside of the operating room a fire extinguisher rated 2-A:10-B:C and complies with 8.1.6 shall be located outside of the room and within 3 feet (0.9 m) of the door or in compliance with 8.2. As discussed during the inspection, the EST- 3 fire alarm panel intended for zoning all the alarm initiating devices, that is in turn connected through the general alarm and trouble relays to the EST- 3, FACP dedicated for Central Station signaling only, is not suitable for Central Station monitoring applications. The EST- 3, FACP is not serving as a Listed NFPA72 system for Central Station applications, nor has it been documented in the EST- 3, installation manual as previously tested and determined compatible for the application. The following options are available to rectify the discrepancy: Install a Listed, intended for the application/compatible DACT module in the primary FACP.The DACT module must be documented device that is Listed for use with manufacturer's equipment. -Or- Cancel this Certificated and reissue the Certificate as a Local or Remote Station Fire Alarm Certificate, with optional offsite monitoring. If your company does not currently maintain the UL Local Fire Alarm installing category, it could be obtained if desired.

As discussed during the inspection, the MXL-IQ fire alarm panel intended for zoning all the alarm initiating devices, that is in turn connected through the general alarm and trouble relays to the Notifier 4011, FACP dedicated for Central Station signaling only, is not suitable for Central Station monitoring applications. The MXL-IQ, FACP is not serving as a Listed NFPA72 system for Central Station applications, nor has it been documented in the MXL-IQ, installation manual as previously tested and determined compatible for the application. The following options are available to rectify the discrepancy: Install a Listed, intended for the application/compatible, DACT module in the primary FACP. The DACT module must be documented device that is Listed for use with manufacturer's equipment. -Or- Cancel this Certificated and reissue the Certificate as a Local or Remote Station Fire Alarm Certificate, with optional offsite monitoring. If your company does not currently maintain the UL Local Fire Alarm installing category, it could be obtained if desired.

The test and inspection reports shall be updated to conform with the test record requirements of the currently adopted edition of the NFPA 72 Chapter 10. 10.1.4 The requirements of this chapter shall apply to both new and existing systems. 10.6.2.3* A record of all inspections, testing, and maintenance shall be provided that includes the following information regarding tests and all the applicable information requested in Figure 10.6.2.3: (1) Date (2) Test frequency (3) Name of property (4) Address (5) Name of person performing inspection, maintenance, tests, or combination thereof, and affiliation, business address, and telephone number (6) Name, address, and representative of approving agency(ies). (7) Designation of the detector(s) tested, for example, Tests performed in accordance with Section __________. (8) Functional test of detectors (9) Functional test of required sequence of operations (10) Check of all smoke detectors. (11) Loop resistance for all fixed-temperature, line-type heat detectors. (12) Other tests as required by the equipment manufacturer’s published instructions. (13) Other tests as required by the authority having jurisdiction. (14) Signatures of tester and approved authority representative. (15) Disposition of problems identified during test (e.g., owner notified, problem corrected/successfully retested, device abandoned in place).

On 10/5/2015 a Fire Service Test was conducted at this location, with the fire marshal present, to test the supervising station retransmission and notification to the Subscriber and Alarm Service Company. An alarm signal was generated at 12:57 pm. A building owner representative was called at 1:01 pm. The fire department was dispatched at 1:04pm. The alarm company service company was not notified and no runner arrived. Several non-compliance issues are noted, per NFPA 72 section 26.3.8.1.2, the Central station is required to retransmit the alarm to the dispatch center as first step (within 90 seconds), then notify the runner service, then notify the subscriber. Please reply with the cause of the service test failure and corrective action taken.

Per UL 827, 8th Edition, Par. 50.1.1: When the retransmission of signals to public safety organizations, such as Public Safety Answering Points (PSAP) or directly to emergency service organizations, (such as fire departments, law enforcement departments and emergency medical service organizations and the like) cannot be completed within 10 minutes or the safety organization has provided notice they are not accepting calls for service, the central station shall take the following actions. A) Without delay, initiate a review of all of the pending signals from monitored alarm systems to identify those which require retransmission to the affected public safety organizations. B) Prioritize all outstanding signals from affected alarm systems by the type of signal fire alarm, emergency call system, hold or panic, medical emergency including carbon monoxide, burglar alarms with line security, burglar alarms without line security, supervisory signals, trouble signals and proceed to have all available on duty personnel notify the individuals named on the call list, leaving messages as described in © where direct contact with those individuals cannot be made. Where it is determined notification for all affected alarm systems cannot be made within 20 minutes, initiate contacting personnel that can support the central station in timely notification(s). (See 19.2, 28.2, and 43.2 as appropriate); c) When an attempt to contact an individual on the call list is not successful, a message shall be left where possible indicating the reason for the call and an attempt to contact the next person on the call list shall be made. If after there has been an attempt to contact everyone on the call list and no one on the call list was reached and no messages could be left, another attempt shall be made using the same or an alternate method within 8 hours from the last attempt; d) The instructions specified in 37.7 apply to signals from non-fire alarm systems. Emergency Communications Systems and Fire alarm systems shall be handled in accordance with National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code, NFPA 72 requirements; and e) A record of all calls and the results shall be created. Exception: Where an authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) over the type of alarm service provided to a protected property has established, in advance or provided instructions for alternative actions during the impairment, the steps noted above may be modified. The policy or instruction shall be part of the record for this event.
Trouble signals shall be programmed to be audible at the control panel. Ref NFPA 72 Chapter 1.5.4.6.1.

UL827 9th Edition, section 8.5.1 All areas of the station (See 1.12) that are not continuously occupied by alarm service company personnel shall be protected by any of the following systems: a) An automatic fire extinguishing system, b) A fire suppression system, or c) An automatic fire detection system 8.5.2 Any of the systems referenced in 8.5.1 and used in such areas shall be connected to a fire-alarm system installed in accordance with NFPA 72, and shall annunciate alarm, supervisory, and trouble conditions in the operating room. * Current Fire Alarm does not meet the requirement. Please describe how you intend to meet this requirement.

When the retransmission of signals to public safety organizations, such as Public Safety Answering Points (PSAP) or directly to emergency service organizations, (such as fire departments, law enforcement departments and emergency medical service organizations and the like) cannot be completed within 10 minutes or the safety organization has provided notice they are not accepting calls for service, the central station shall take the following actions. a) Without delay, initiate a review of all of the pending signals from monitored alarm systems to identify those which require retransmission to the affected public safety organizations. b) Prioritize all outstanding signals from affected alarm systems by the type of signal fire alarm, emergency call system, hold or panic, medical emergency including carbon monoxide, burglar alarms with line security, burglar alarms without line security, supervisory signals, trouble signals and proceed to have all available on duty personnel notify the individuals named on the call list, leaving messages as described in (c) where direct contact with those individuals cannot be made. Where it is determined notification for all affected alarm systems cannot be made within 20 minutes, initiate contacting personnel that can support the central station in timely notification(s). (See 19.2, 28.2, and 43.2 as appropriate); c) When an attempt to contact an individual on the call list is not successful, a message shall be left where possible indicating the reason for the call and an attempt to contact the next person on the call list shall be made. If after there has been an attempt to contact everyone on the call list and no one on the call list was reached and no messages could be left, another attempt shall be made using the same or an alternate method within 8 hours from the last attempt; d) The instructions specified in 37.7 apply to signals from non-fire alarm systems. Emergency Communications Systems and Fire alarm systems shall be handled in accordance with National Fire Alarm and Signaling Code, NFPA 72 requirements; and e) A record of all calls and the results shall be created. Exception: Where an authority having jurisdiction (AHJ) over the type of alarm service provided to a protected property has established, in advance or provided instructions for alternative actions during the impairment, the steps noted above may be modified. The policy or instruction shall be part of the record for this event. Refer to UL 827 (8th Edition) par. 50.1.1.

Insulated conductors and cables installed in these enclosures or raceways in underground installations shall comply with 310.10©. NFPA 70, section 310.10© Insulated conductors and cables used in wet locations shall comply with one of the following: (1) Be moisture-impervious metal-sheathed (2) Be types MTW, RHW, RHW-2, TW, THW, THW-2, THHW, THWN, THWN-2, XHHW, XHHW-2, or ZW (3) Be of a type listed for use in wet locations **Wires for PIV supervision shall be listed for underground use.

NFPA 72, edition 23.8.5.11.1 Automatic fire suppression system alarm– initiating devices and supervisory signal-initiating devices and their circuits shall be designed and installed so that they cannot be subject to tampering, opening, or removal without initiating a signal. This provision shall include junction boxes installed outside of buildings to facilitate access to the initiating device circuit. Exception No. 2: Tamper–resistant screws or other approved mechanical means shall be permitted for preventing access to junction boxes and device covers installed outside of buildings.

Nonmetallic-Sheathed Cable. In both exposed and concealed locations where nonmetallic-sheathed cables pass through either factory- or field-punched, cut, or drilled slots or holes in metal members, the cable shall be protected by listed bushings or listed grommets covering all metal edges that are securely fastened in the opening prior to installation of the cable.Ref. NFPA 70 Article 300.4 Nonmetalic-Sheathed Cable.
